order_string: tell me about the citizens for prosperity|what are the citizens for prosperity|citizens for prosperity|explain the citizens for prosperity|the cfp
success_message: The Citizens for Prosperity, commander. A political faction born in the Pyro system, driven by the dream of establishing some semblance of order there. In the middle of that chaos abandoned to the gangs, the CFP presents itself as the civilised answer, the one promising security, stable trade and protection for honest people. On paper, an almost soothing programme. In practice it is an armed militia doubled with a local administration, locking down its territory through force and control of resources. They hold a few Pyro stations and outposts, which they work to rebuild and run as genuinely durable infrastructure. Their rhetoric celebrates order and prosperity; their methods slide frankly towards authoritarianism. With the system's pirates and criminal organisations they keep electric relations, fighting them as much as disputing ground with them. Some observers see nothing there but a paramilitary regime dressed up as a citizens' movement. It is impossible to settle: saviours of Pyro, or simply a gang in better costume? In a place of that calibre, commander, the line between order and tyranny hangs by a thread.

order_string: tell me about alien week|what is alien week|alien week|explain alien week|the alien week
success_message: Alien Week, commander: an annual imperial celebration devoted to the alien cultures allied with or integrated into the UEE. It draws its soul from First Contact, that founding moment of 12 June 2438 when humanity met the Banu and stopped being alone in the cosmos. For the duration of the event, the empire turns its spotlights on its stellar neighbours: the trading Banu, the Xi'an in all their refinement, the Tevarin with their wounded history. On the programme: cultural exhibitions, alien cuisine, historical talks, technology demonstrations and grand diplomatic ceremonies. For shipbuilders it is also the ideal moment to showcase ships born of alien technology, or inspired by it. Unlike First Contact Day, which recalls one precise fact, Alien Week honours more broadly the whole web of ties woven between humanity and the other peoples of the verse. It is a celebration of understanding, maintained in spite of yesterday's wars and the ever-live Vanduul threat. A reminder, commander, that the empire only spread by learning to extend a hand rather than a fist. At least… when the other side agreed to it.

order_string: tell me about the crusader blockade|what is the crusader blockade|the nine tails lockdown|explain the crusader blockade|the crusader lockdown
success_message: The Blockade of Crusader, commander, also known as the Nine Tails Lockdown: one of the most audacious strokes a gang has ever dared in the Stanton system. The operation was orchestrated by the Nine Tails, who fielded a genuinely structured fleet, destroyers, fighters and interceptors, in order to sever the trade routes around Crusader and its stations. Their aim: lock down civilian and industrial traffic, ransom whatever could be ransomed, and strike at the very heart of Crusader Industries' economy. While the siege lasted, freighters were boarded, civilians trapped, and shortages set in around the besieged stations. This had left simple piracy behind, commander, and entered the realm of a show of force, a challenge thrown in the face of corporate authority. It took raising the Civilian Defence Force and a swarm of armed civilian pilots to break the blockade and clear the lanes. For several days, that ordinarily flourishing corner of Stanton turned into a battlefield. The affair laid bare the failings of corporate security in front of a determined criminal organisation. Proof, commander, that at the very heart of the empire, order hangs by a thread.

order_string: tell me about the headhunters|what are the headhunters|the headhunters|explain the headhunters|headhunters
success_message: The Headhunters, commander. A structured criminal gang, expert in the coldest end of piracy. You find them in poorly guarded systems and border zones, all around Pyro and the illegal trade routes. Where the ordinary pirate plunders as luck allows, they act on order: tracking, interception, targeted elimination of pilots or valuable convoys. Assassinations, recovery of very specific cargoes, contracts placed on a head, intimidation — it is a service, not simple looting. They fly fast fighters and cobbled-together craft, shaped for ambush and the lightning strike. Their name comes from a sinister reputation: bringing back their target's head to attest the contract was honoured to the letter. Security records regularly tie them to criminal networks of an altogether different scale. These are not brutes given over to chaos, commander, but craftsmen of violence. And if your name lands on one of their lists, best to learn of it before they do.

order_string: tell me about the krthak|what are the krthak|the krthak|explain the krthak|krthak
success_message: The Kr'thak, commander: very likely the most enigmatic alien civilisation the verse knows, and the least well understood. Where humanity has been able to approach the Banu, the Tevarin or even the Xi'an, with them direct contact is all but nonexistent. The little we know comes from ancient Xi'an archives and a few scattered diplomatic reports; the empire's own intelligence services are desperately short of solid data. They are said to be an ancient and immense power, established well beyond the known human frontier. Their most resounding deed goes far back: a war of scale, appallingly destructive, waged long ago against the Xi'an, a conflict that left a lasting mark on the latter. There is practically no official description of the Kr'thak; even their appearance remains largely unknown, which is enough to feed every rumour and every conjecture. The rare accounts hint at a species of very advanced technology, of a size to rival the Xi'an on military as well as economic ground. They maintain a calculated isolation and watch jealously over their borders, keeping their designs to themselves. For the empire, commander, the Kr'thak remain a vast unknown — and an unknown capable of standing up to the Xi'an commands respect. Let us simply hope we never have to make the introductions.

order_string: tell me about rough and ready|what is rough and ready|rough and ready|explain rough and ready|the rough and ready faction
success_message: Rough & Ready, commander. An armed industrial faction of the Pyro system, and probably the most respectable in the whole sector — which, out there, does not weigh very much. It is a gathering of scavengers, mechanics and industrialists, versed in salvage, ship recycling, makeshift repair and the trading of fished-out equipment. They hold several abandoned installations and stations, transformed into genuine industrial strongholds bristling with weapons. Stripping wrecks, moving components, patching craft back together, watching over convoys and worksites: their whole business orbits what others leave behind. Unlike the local gangs and pirates, Rough & Ready bets on profit and on a certain stability in the area's economy. They trade with just about everyone, so long as the deal pays and nobody muddies the water. Their fleet, cobbled together piece by piece from recovered scrap, looks improvised — but it proves formidably effective. Many hold them to be one of the few factions able to sustain a semblance of durable industrial order in the middle of Pyro's chaos. If fate obliges you to deal with someone in that sector, commander, they are probably the least unpredictable. And at Pyro, believe me, that is very nearly a compliment.
